sections.title: Introduction to Linux Migration Compass
sections.intro:
Originally Published: 9th April, 2025
Updated: 16th October, 2025

The Linux Migration Compass is a comprehensive guide designed to help users new to Linux transition seamlessly to the Pop!_OS 22.04 Linux distribution. Pop!_OS by system76 is a Linux distribution known for its polished interface, performance optimizations and easy handling of hybrid graphics. The guide will cover everything from installation to advanced usage, ensuring that you have all the information you need to make the switch, and familiarise with Linux successfully.

blocks.value: Pop!_OS by system76 is a Linux distribution known for its polished interface, performance optimizations, automatic handling of hybrid graphics. It is designed for developers, engineers, and computer scientists. It features a custom GNOME desktop environment and a focus on productivity. Thus making it one of the best operating systems that suits a majority of users.

sections.title: Creating a Bootable Live Disk
sections.intro: The following guide describes how to download the Pop!_OS.iso image, write it to a flash drive, and boot into the OS Installation setup.

blocks.value:
Before starting, ensure you have met the following requirements:
-An x86 Computer with at least 4gb RAM and 20gb free storage
-A USB drive with at least 8 GB of storage.
- A stable internet connection.
- At least 20 GB of free space on your hard drive.
- A backup of all important data on your Windows system.
Back up your data to an external drive or cloud storage (e.g., Google Drive, OneDrive) before proceeding. Partitioning can lead to data loss if not done correctly.
Visit https://pop.system76.com and download the latest ISO file. Choose the version that matches your hardware (e.g., Intel/AMD or NVIDIA).
Use a tool like Rufus https://rufus.ie to create a bootable USB drive:

1. Insert your USB drive.
    2. Open Rufus.
    3. Select the downloaded Pop!_OS ISO file.
    4. Choose your USB drive as the target.
    5. Click "START" to create the bootable USB.
This process will erase all data on the USB drive. Ensure you’ve backed up any important files from the USB.
Insert your USB drive into your computer.

1. Restart your computer.
    2. Enter the BIOS/UEFI settings by pressing the appropriate key (usually ESC, F2, F12, or DEL, check your manufacturer’s documentation).
    3. Set the USB drive as the first boot device in the boot order. (This is a safety step to reboot into the USB without any extra steps if necessary)
    4. Save changes and reboot.
Alternatively you may be prompted to select a boot device when you attempt to enter BIOS settings.
Your computer should boot from the USB drive, and you’ll see the Pop!_OS installation screen.
Ensure Secure Boot is disabled in your BIOS settings to install Pop!_OS. BIOS settings vary by manufacturer, so refer to your computer’s documentation for instructions.

sections.title: Installing Pop!_OS
sections.intro: This section will guide you through the installation process of Pop!_OS on your computer once we have booted from our live disk

blocks.value: Once you boot from the USB drive, you will see the Pop!_OS welcome screen. Here you have booted into a Live Session where you can try Pop!_OS without installing it by selecting "Try Demo Mode" or proceed with the installation by selecting "Install Pop!_OS". You can explore and use the OS without any risk to your current system. I recommend you familiarize yourself with the OS before proceeding with the installation.
Once you are ready to install, select "Install Pop!_OS"

1. Select your language
    2. Select your locale
    3. Choose your keyboard layout
    4. Select either from the installation type: "Clean Install" or "Custom (Advanced)"

If you have just one disk and would like to dual-boot, choose "Custom (Advanced)", select the partition where you want to install Pop!_OS. You can create, delete, or resize partitions as needed, you will need to reboot into the live session if you have made any new partitions as target. For a clean install, select "Clean Install" and follow the prompts to erase the choice of disk and install Pop!_OS.
blocks.message: At this step you can decide to dual-boot between Windows and Linux or install Pop!_OS as the only OS

blocks.value: This step is destructive and will erase the chosen partition/disk chosen, make sure you have selected the intended target space to avoid losing any existing data or your Windows. If you are dual-booting, ensure you have enough space on your hard drive for Pop!_OS.
5. Click "Erase and Install" after you have chosen your installation type
After the installation type, you will be prompted to create a user account. Enter your name and user name, then you will be prompted to set a password. This will be your primary account on Pop!_OS.
This password will be used to log in to your account as well as for performing high privilege tasks throughout the OS.

You will be prompted to enable full disk encryption. This is optional but recommended for security. If you choose to enable it, you will need to enter a password each time you boot your computer. You can choose to set the password to be the same as your user account password or a different one. This is a security feature that encrypts your entire disk, preventing unauthorized access. If you choose not to enable it, you can skip this step.

Once the installation is complete, you will be prompted to restart your computer. Remove the USB drive when prompted during the restart process. Your computer will boot into Pop!_OS, and you will be greeted with the login screen. Enter your user account credentials to log in.
Congratulations!, You have successfully installed Pop!_OS on your computer.

sections.title: Welcome to Pop!_OS
sections.intro: Welcome to Pop!_OS! This section will guide you through the initial setup, configuration and familiarization of your new operating system.

subtopics.title: Keeping your system up to date

blocks.value:
The most frequent task you will perform on your system is keeping it up to date. Pop!_OS is based on Ubuntu, which means it uses the APT package manager to manage software updates. It is recommended to update your computer on a daily basis to ensure the latest software and security updates are installed.

Later in this documentation we will learn how to add additional sources and package managers. You can update your system using the terminal or the Pop!_Shop, Pop's own GUI for installing and updating softwares and applications.
Click on the top right corner of your screen to open the system tray. Click on Wi-Fi, and connect to your Wi-Fi network.
Open your terminal and run the following commands.

sudo apt update
sudo apt upgrade

You will observe a list of packages that are available for update. You will be prompted to enter your password. Enter your password and press Enter. The system will then download and install the updates. Ideally reboot your system after the updates are installed to ensure all changes take effect.

commands starting with sudo are run with superuser privileges and will require authentication with every use. This means you have elevated permissions to perform administrative tasks. Be cautious when using sudo, as it can modify system files and settings.

Once you have installed Pop!_OS, take some time to familiarize yourself with the OS. The Pop!_OS desktop environment is based on GNOME, which provides a clean and modern interface.

Top Bar Located at the top of the screen, this bar contains your system tray, clock, and notification area. It serves as a quick-glance status panel for essential functions and updates. On the left edge, the “Applications” button gives you access to all installed applications. This is similar to the Windows Start Menu's application list. Clicking the date and time reveals a combined calendar and notification tray, making it easy to track events and system alerts in one view.

System tray This section on the right side of the top bar houses controls for screen brightness, volume, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and system power options. If you have an NVIDIA GPU, you'll also find GPU mode options here, these allow switching between integrated and discrete graphics for power or performance, which will be explained in more detail later in the guide.

Launcher Accessed by pressing the Super [Windows] key or clicking the search icon in the dock. It is a powerful tool that you can use to search files by typing 'find ', search on the web by typing 'google/ddg ', open applications and view currently opened applications and switch between them or even solve mathematical expressions!

Bottom Dock Your favorite or pinned applications appear here for quick access. You can customize this dock by right-clicking apps to add or remove them based on your preferences. You may also see a button for opening the launcher, the applications view, and your workspaces.

Settings Accessed via the system tray, the Settings menu will let you configure display, audio, network, user accounts, and more.

Largely, you’ll find the navigation and user experience to be very intuitive, even if you’re new to Linux. Apps like LibreOffice and Files serve as user-friendly alternatives to Microsoft Office and Windows File Explorer, helping you transition seamlessly.

Pop!_OS comes with a variety of pre-installed applications, but you may want to install additional software. You can do this using the Pop!_Shop or the terminal. The Pop!_Shop is the software center where you can find and install applications. It is similar to the Microsoft Store or Apple App Store.
To install software using the terminal, use the following command

sudo apt update
sudo apt install <package-name>
Or to install multiple packages at once, use the following command:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install <package-name> <package-name> ...
Replace <package-name> with the name of the software you want to install. For example, to install VLC media player, use the command:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install vlc
we run "sudo apt update" to ensure we have the latest package lists before installing any software.
You can also search for software in the Pop!_Shop by typing the name of the software in the search bar.

There are a variety of software distributions available for Pop!_OS. A well known one being Flatpak. Flatpak is a software utility for software virtualization and deployment. It allows you to install and run a wide variety of applications, often reliably. Flatpak comes preinstalled with Pop!_OS
flatpak install <application-name>

When roughly searching for an application, flatpak will give you a list of matches, enter the number corresponding to the application you want to install, and choose whether to install it system-wide or just for your user.
blocks.message: About installing Flatpak applications via terminal

blocks.value: One of the most common ways to install softwares is through the terminal. The commands vary depending on your choice of softwares or package managers (Follow instructions from the respective software provider). For example, to install Chrome (although you should really consider Gecko based browsers like Zen and Floorp!)
deb http://dl.google.com/linux/chrome/deb/ stable main
Here we added google's repository to our sources list.
wget -q -O - https://dl-ssl.google.com/linux/linux_signing_key.pub | sudo apt-key add -
Here we validated the repository by adding google's signing key to our apt keyring.
And finally, we can install Chrome by running the following command:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install google-chrome-stable

You may want to consider using a browser like Floorp or Zen, which are Gecko based browsers that focus on privacy (out-of-the box unlike Mozilla's Firefox -_-), performance, and each has a unique experience, Floorp has great functionality for workspaces, while Zen excells in a sleek, compact and minimal design. To install Floorp, run the following commands:

curl -fsSL https://ppa.floorp.app/KEY.gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/Floorp.gpg
sudo curl -sS --compressed -o /etc/apt/sources.list.d/Floorp.list 'https://ppa.floorp.app/Floorp.list'
sudo apt update
sudo apt install floorp
To install Zen:
flatpak install zen browser # try your best to install it as we discussed earlier!

@ Online Accounts, under the settings app allows you to connect your other accounts like Google or Microsoft to your Linux system allowing several apps to easily integrate your online accounts for services such as emails, calendars, cloud storage, messaging and etc.

sections.title: Advanced Customization and Tuning
sections.intro: In this section, we will explore advanced customization options and performance tuning for Pop!_OS. This includes configuring the desktop environment, optimizing system performance, and customizing the user interface to suit your preferences.

blocks.value: Setting up a facial biometric authentication is a great way to enhance the security and convenience of using your system. It replaces the need to manually enter your password for elevated privileges or logging in. If your device has a Webcam or an IR camera. You can easily set up your facial biometric authentication using Howdy

# Adding Howdy's repository to our sources
sudo add-apt-repository -y ppa:boltgolt/howdy
Now we can install Howdy by running the following command:
You will be prompted to choose between different options for howdy. f, b, s for Fast, Balanced or Secure. You may choose that best fit your needs.

sudo apt update
sudo apt install howdy ffmpeg
We are installing ffmpeg for troubleshooting purposes, testing out our available cameras and their respective video formats.
Before we can add our face to Howdy, we need to set up howdy to identify and correctly use our webcam. Run the following command to see a list of your camera sensors
ls /dev | grep "video."
Replace <videoX> with the name of your camera sensor. For example, if your camera sensor is /dev/video0, run the following command:
ffplay /dev/<videoX>
Test out each of the video sources and see which one outputs the actual camera feed. Take note of it.
If you are using an IR sensor, there is a known bug which prevents Howdy from using it. See the solution below to resolve at this current time.

Howdy outputs an unsupported image format error for most IR camera sensors, this is due to an issue with numpy version 2 not resolving well with the current Howdy version. To resolve this, we need to downgrade numpy to a compatible version. The issue is brought up here: https://stackoverflow.com/a/78638053
sudo pip install --upgrade numpy==1.26.4
Once you have identified the correct camera sensor, you can add your face to Howdy by opening the Howdy configuration file. Run the following command:
sudo howdy config
In the configuration file, find the line
device_path = /dev/v4l/by-path/none
Replace the path with the correct camera we identified earlier /dev/<videoX>
device_path = /dev/<videoX>
Click CTRL-X and then Y to save and exit the configuration file. And now we are ready to add our face to Howdy.
sudo howdy add

If your device has a fingerprint reader, you can set up fingerprint authentication using Fprint. Fprint is a D-Bus service to access fingerprint readers. It is supported by most modern fingerprint readers.
Most HP laptops and other brands unfortunately may not support fingerprint readers on linux. To check if you have a supported fingerprint reader, run the following commands:
lsusb
Try to rule out devices that mention anything other than fingerprint, and look for "Goodix", "ElanTech", "Synaptics" or "Validity". Example:
Bus 001 Device 003: ID 138a:00ab Validity Sensors, Inc.

Visit https://fprint.freedesktop.org/supported-devices.html and try finding your fingerprint's name and ID in this case, the name is "Validity Sensors" and the ID is "138a:00ab".

If you are not able to find yours in the list, your fingerprint reader is likely not supported.
If you are able to find your fingerprint reader in the list, you can set it up by installing Fprint:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install fprintd libpam-fprintd
Next, we need to enroll our fingerprint. after running this command, place your finger on the fingerprint reader when prompted.
fprintd-enroll
Now we update the system to use our fingerprint reader for authentication. This can now be used to unlock your screen, authenticate sudo commands, and log in to your system.
sudo pam-auth-update

Pop!_OS uses GNOME as its desktop environment, which is highly customizable. You can change the appearance of your desktop, including themes, icons, and fonts. To customize your desktop environment, you can use GNOME Tweaks. Install it using the following command:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install gnome-tweaks

Once installed, you can open GNOME Tweaks from the applications menu. Here you can change the appearance of your desktop, including themes, icons, and fonts to your liking. You can also enable or disable extensions, which are small add-ons that enhance the functionality of GNOME. Many of these add-ons are available on the GNOME Extensions website: https://extensions.gnome.org as well as Pop!_OS's own Pop!_Shop.

Under settings and GNOME Tweaks, you can also change the behavior of your desktop environment, including window management, workspaces, keyboard shortcuts, and touchpad and gesture settings to your liking

A Display Server Protocol is a communication protocol between the display server and the applications. It is responsible for rendering graphics on the screen and handling input events from the keyboard and mouse. Pop!_OS uses X11 as its default display server protocol, which is an older protocol that has been around for decades. However, it is still widely used and supported by most applications.

If you are using a laptop with a trackpad and want to take advantage of the latest features, you can switch to Wayland, which is a newer display server protocol that is designed to be faster, efficient and secure. This will also enable most advanced gestures and in-app gestures that X11 does not support on its own. To switch to Wayland run the following commands:

If you have an older PC with NVIDIA graphics or an older laptop, wayland may not perform well on your device, and you may want to stick with X11. You can always switch back to X11 if you experience any issues with Wayland.
sudo nano /etc/gdm3/custom.conf

This will open your GNOME Display Manager configuration file. Find the line that says #WaylandEnable=false and uncomment it by removing the # at the beginning of the line. And write true. Save and exit the file.
Now log out of your current session with CTRL + ALT + DEL or through the system tray.
Ensure you have saved any work as this will log you out and close all of your applications
On your login page, a gear icon should appear on the bottom right corner, click it, and select Wayland. This setting will persist and all of your sessions will use Wayland from now on.
Wayland has known issues specifically with a discrete NVIDIA GPU, in the next section we will cover any issues you may encounter and how to resolve them.

sections.title: System Management and Troubleshooting
sections.intro: In this section, we will cover system management tips and troubleshooting techniques for Pop!_OS. This includes managing system resources, monitoring performance, and troubleshooting common issues.

blocks.value:
If your system has discrete NVIDIA graphics, depending on the chosen installation in the beginning, Pop!_OS will work out of the box saving a ton of hassle for you. Pop!_OS has excellent hybrid GPU support, allowing you customize and choose what either of your GPUs (Integrated or NVIDIA) do.

To check if your NVIDIA drivers are installed, run the following command:
nvidia-smi
You can always run the following command to download or update your NVIDIA drivers if necessary.
sudo apt install system76-driver-nvidia
To download a specific version of NVIDIA drivers, you can use the following command and replace xxx with the desired release version:
sudo apt install nvidia-driver-xxx

Most systems with hybrid GPUs may experience a black screen once waking up from suspend. This is a well known and notorious issue mainly caused by NVIDIA drivers. There are no permanents fixes to date, however they are some workarounds you can try:
As for September 2025, this issue has largly been resolved with the latest NVIDIA drivers. If you are still experiencing this issue, try updating your NVIDIA drivers to the latest version.
1. Updating NVIDIA drivers to the latest version
sudo apt install nvidia-driver-580 # as for September 2025, 570 drivers are also stable.

2. Making sure your display refresh rate in settings matches the specification of your laptop or PC's refresh rate. Wayland can provide you more refresh rates than what your monitor is desgined to run by default.

According to what I have investigated with journalctl logs, the graphics driver whether Intel's or NVIDIA's fails to reinitialize the display output due to a mismatch in the default refresh rate of the monitor with the selected one in settings.

3. If none of these solutions work then unfortunately there isn't much you can do. You may turn off your NVIDIA GPU under your BIOS settings and enable only when needed and avoid suspending your laptop.
Pop!_OS provides several tools for managing system resources and monitoring performance.

In applications, you can find the System Monitor application, which provides a graphical interface for monitoring system resources such as CPU, memory, and disk usage. You can also use the terminal to monitor system resources using commands like:
top

There are many other system monitoring apps you can download that provide more advanced features such as temperature and frequency readings in a highly customisable terminal window. Some popular ones include:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install lm-sensors
sudo sensors-detect # Follow the prompts to detect your sensors
btop and i7z are also great tools for monitoring system resources in depth.

sudo apt update
sudo apt install btop i7z
To monitor your Intel integrated GPU, and the package power, install the following package:

sudo apt update && sudo apt install intel-gpu-tools
sudo intel_gpu_top # to monitor the iGPU in real-time

Pop!_OS supports hardware acceleration for certain applications, such as web browsers and video players. Hardware acceleration allows these applications to use the GPU for rendering graphics, which can significantly improve power consumption, saving tons of battery life on laptops.
To enable hardware acceleration in chromium browsers, you can go to Settings > Advanced > System and enable the option for "Use hardware acceleration when available".

To enable hardware acceleraton in Gecko based browsers (Firefox and etc), you can go to Settings > General > Performance and enable the option for "Use recommended performance settings". Usually this may not work for Firefox, and you may need to enable it manually by going to about:config and setting the following options:
layers.acceleration.force-enabled = true
gfx.webrender.all = true
By default Pop!_OS does not come with certain media codecs preinstalled due to licensing issues. However, you can easily install them using the following command:
sudo apt install -y ubuntu-restricted-extras gstreamer1.0-plugins-bad

To confirm whether hardware acceleration is working, use the intel_gpu_top package as we learned earlier. Observe movement in the Video section, any bars appearing there indicate hardware acceleration is enabled.

Pop!_OS is one of the best linux distribution in battery efficiency out there. It has excellent battery management systems ensuring high power delivery when needed and high efficiency optimizations when needed, and provides several tools for managing battery life and optimizing power consumption through its power profiles and discrete GPU handling. It will most certainly bring a new life to your laptop on battery performance compared to Windows. You can use the Power settings in the Settings menu to adjust your power options, such as profiles, screen brightness and sleep settings etc.

System76 recommends not installing any additional battery saving tools or applications, as Pop!_OS already has built-in power management features that are optimized for the hardware. Installing additional tools may cause conflicts and reduce battery life.

To monitor battery usage and performance, you can install powertop, an excellent monitoring tool that provides in-depth breakdowns of power consumption throughout the system's softwares and hardwares:
sudo apt install powertop

The terminal is a powerful tool in Pop!_OS that allows you to interact with the system using commands. It is a great way to perform tasks quickly and efficiently. In Linux you may find yourself using the terminal more often than you would in Windows. The terminal is a command-line interface that allows you to interact with the operating system using text commands. It is a powerful tool that can be used to perform a wide variety of tasks, including file management, system administration, and software installation. To open the terminal, press Super + T or search for "Terminal" in the applications menu.
To navigate the file system, use the following commands:
cd <directory> # Change directory
cd ~ # Change to home directory
ls # List files in the current directory
pwd # Print current directory path
To create, delete, and manage files and directories, use the following commands:
mkdir <directory> # Create a new directory
rm <file> # Remove a file
rmdir <directory> # Remove a directory
cp <source> <destination> # Copy a file or directory
mv <source> <destination> # Move or rename a file or directory
touch <file> # Create a new empty file
cat <file> # Display the contents of a file
nano <file> # Edit a file using nano text editor
To search for files and directories, use the following commands:
find <directory> -name <file> # Search for a file in a directory
grep <pattern> <file> # Search for a pattern in a file
To manage system processes, use the following commands:
ps # List running processes
kill <pid> # Kill a process by its PID
pkill <process_name> # Kill a process by its name

Sometimes you may get an error saying permission denied, for such errors they require elevated privileges. Prefix your commands with sudo in this case. For example, to remove a file with elevated privileges, run the following command:
sudo rm <file>

sections.title: Applications and Alternatives
sections.intro: Linux has a large community of amazing developers creating and managing powerful, open-sourced applications. In this section, we will cover some of the most popular applications and alternatives available for Pop!_OS. This includes productivity tools, media players, and development tools.

blocks.value: Pop_OS is a great platform for gaming, with support for a wide range of games and gaming platforms. One of the most popular gaming platforms on Linux is Steam, which provides access to a large library of games. To install Steam, run the following command:
sudo apt install steam

Another popular gaming platform is Lutris, which is an open-source gaming platform that allows you to manage and play games from various sources, including Steam, GOG, and Battle.net. To install Lutris, run the following command:
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:lutris-team/lutris
And finally download Lutris:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install lutris
Discord is a popular communication platform for gamers and communities. Discord can be installed through Flatpak by running the following command:
flatpak install flathub com.discordapp.Discord
Gaming is easier than ever before on linux, Learn more about it at https://www.protondb.com/, https://www.linuxgamingcentral.com/ and https://support.system76.com/articles/linux-gaming/
One of the most well known photo editing software is GIMP, renowned for its powerful and advanced tools. You can learn more about them at https://www.gimp.org/
sudo apt install gimp
A simpler alternative to GIMP is Pinta, a simplier but capable photo editing software. Learn more about them at https://www.pinta-project.com/. Add the repository:
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:pinta-maintainers/pinta-stable
Download Pinta

sudo apt update
sudo apt install pinta

DaVinci Resolve is the most commonly known professional video editing software on Linux. It offers both a free version and a paid Studio version with additional features. To download and set up DaVinci Resolve, follow these steps:

1. Download from https://www.blackmagicdesign.com
    /products/davinciresolve
    2. Click on the zip file "DaVinci_Resolve_XX.Y.Z_Linux.zip" and extract it.
    3. Click "DaVinci_Resolve_XX.Y.Z_Linux.run" to run the setup.
    4. Follow the instructions as prompted.
DaVinci Resolve requires proper OpenCL drivers to function. Pop!_OS includes compatible drivers for most modern AMD, Intel, and NVIDIA GPUs. Make sure your system is up to date before installation.

The free version does not support decoding or encoding H.264 and H.265 video codecs, regardless of container type. Neither the free nor Studio version supports AAC audio codec. However, the free version does support MP4 containers with compatible codecs like AV1 video with MP3 or PCM audio.
To work with unsupported formats, you'll need to transcode them using FFmpeg, which we installed earlier in this guide. FFmpeg is a powerful tool for converting video and audio file formats.
For DaVinci Resolve Free, if your MP4 contains H.264/H.265 video with MP3 or PCM audio, transcode only the video to a supported codec:
ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -c:v dnxhd -profile:v dnxhr_hq -pix_fmt yuv422p -c:a copy output.mov
If your MP4 contains H.264/H.265 video with AAC audio, transcode both video and audio:
ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -c:v dnxhd -profile:v dnxhr_hq -pix_fmt yuv422p -c:a alac output.mov
For DaVinci Resolve Studio, H.264 and H.265 are supported, but AAC audio is not. You can transcode just the audio while keeping the video intact:
ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -c:v copy -c:a alac output.mov
For converting audio files to a compatible format for either version:
ffmpeg -i input.mp3 -c:a pcm_s16le output.wav
Follow this amazing guide at https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/DaVinci_Resolve for further information.

Another powerful video editing tool is Kdenlive, which is a free and open-source video editing software. It is a great alternative to Davinci Resolve and is available for Linux. Kdenlive can be downloaded from their site at https://kdenlive.org/en/download/. To install via terminal, add the repository:
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:kdenlive/kdenlive-stable
And finally download Kdenlive:

sudo apt update
sudo apt install kdenlive

One of the most popular video players on Linux is VLC Media Player. It is a free and open-source media player that supports a wide range of audio and video formats. To install VLC, run the following command:
sudo apt install vlc
Another popular media player is MPV, which is a free and open-source media player that is known for its simplicity and performance. Install via terminal using the following command:
sudo apt install mpv

System76 has packed up all things machine learning and deep learning into a single package called Tensorman. It is a collection of tools and libraries for machine learning and deep learning. It includes popular libraries such as TensorFlow, PyTorch, and Keras, making ML/DL extremely easy to set up and use. To install Tensorman, run the following command:

sudo apt install tensorman
sudo apt install nvidia-docker2 # For NVIDIA CUDA Support

sections.title: Your Linux Journey
sections.intro: Congratulations! You have successfully installed and configured Pop!_OS on your system. You are now ready to explore the world of Linux and take advantage of its powerful features and capabilities. Remember that Linux is a community-driven operating system, and there are many resources available to help you along the way. Whether you are a beginner or an experienced user, there is always something new to learn and discover in the world of Linux.

blocks.value: System76 are pioneers when it comes to making a very powerful yet efficient modern linux operating systems. Pop!_OS is soon set to see a major update, bringing in CosmicDE, a new desktop environment that is completely written in Rust, and is expected to very strongly compete with two existing mainstream desktop environments; GNOME and KDE. Expecting unparalleled power efficiency as well as a highly snappy and fast OS feel, and keeping up to date the most modern GNOME and KDE customization techniques and tweaks.

If you have any questions or need support, you can visit the Pop!_OS community forums at https://pop.system76.com/ or join the Pop!_OS subreddit at https://www.reddit.com/r/pop_os/. You can also find many tutorials and guides on the official Pop!_OS website at https://pop.system76.com/docs/.

Your journey with Linux is all about learning what you operate and truly having control over your system. It's learning process which may seem inundating at first, but is a rewarding experience. You will find yourself learning new things every day and discovering new tools and applications that can help you be more productive and efficient, and even start to feel more comfortable with using the terminal more frequently. The Linux community is vast and welcoming, and there are many resources available to help you along the way. Regardless of if you are a beginner or an experienced user, there is always something new to learn and discover in the world of Linux.
